---
title: "2.3 Pros and Cons of Mean-Reverting Strategies"
subtitle: "PDF notes | Chapter 02. The Basics of Mean Reversion"

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "NotoSerifCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    mainfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSerifCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    sansfont: "NotoSansCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    sansfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSansCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    monofont: "MapleMono-NF-CN-Regular.ttf"
    monofontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Bold.ttf"
      - "ItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Italic.ttf"
      - "BoldItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-BoldItalic.ttf"
    mathfont: "latinmodern-math.otf"
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    filecolor: blue
include-in-header:
  text: |
    \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{title}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{author}{\rmfamily}
    \addtokomafont{date}{\rmfamily}
---

# Mean Reversion Often Looks Better Than It Feels in a Crisis

Chan ends the chapter by explaining why mean-reversion strategies are so
attractive to traders and so dangerous to underestimate. Their attraction comes
from consistency. When a strategy repeatedly sells temporary overreactions or
buys temporary dislocations, it can produce many small gains, short drawdowns,
and relatively high Sharpe ratios.

These features are valuable in backtests and in live trading. A short time
scale of reversion means more round trips per year, more data for statistical
evaluation, and often a higher compounded growth rate. In ordinary market
conditions, the strategy can appear calm, disciplined, and easy to trust.

# The Advantage Creates a Behavioral Trap

The same consistency that makes mean reversion attractive can create
overconfidence. A trader who sees a long sequence of successful pullbacks can
start treating the strategy as safer than it really is. The equity curve looks
stable. Losing periods are brief. The model appears to confirm itself over and
over again.

This is exactly when leverage tends to rise. The trader feels least threatened
after repeated success, so the strategy is often traded most aggressively at
the point of greatest hidden vulnerability. Chan explicitly invokes the Long
Term Capital Management lesson here: a mean-reversion strategy that has looked
reliably profitable for a long time can still break violently.

# The Structural Risk Is a Breakdown in the Relationship

A mean-reversion trade assumes that the observed deviation is temporary. The
spread, price, or relative relationship is supposed to move back toward its
equilibrium. The danger is that the deviation may instead reflect a genuine
structural change. What looks like a temporary dislocation can turn out to be a
new regime.

When that happens, the strategy's logic works against the trader. The position
is often increased as the deviation grows because a larger deviation looks like
a stronger bargain. In ordinary times that scaling can help. In a breakdown it
magnifies the loss.

# Rare Losses Hurt More Because They Arrive After Success

Chan's warning is not that mean reversion loses a little every day. It is the
opposite. The strategy can win frequently enough to lull the trader into
maximum confidence, and the rare large loss then arrives when position size is
largest.

This is why the downside can be especially severe. A long sequence of ordinary
success does not prepare the trader for a regime change. It often removes the
psychological caution that would have reduced exposure.

# Risk Management Is Harder Than It First Appears

Mean reversion also creates a problem for conventional stop losses. Many
strategies can justify exiting when price keeps moving in the wrong direction.
For a mean-reversion trade, however, an adverse move is often the very
condition that originally justified the entry. The strategy expects stress
before recovery.

That makes ordinary stop-loss rules awkward. A stop that is too tight can kill
the trade precisely when the intended edge is strongest. A stop that is too
loose can permit catastrophic escalation during a true breakdown. Chan
therefore treats risk management for mean reversion as both essential and
specialized, deferring the full treatment to Chapter 8.

# The Right Conclusion Is Respect, Not Rejection

The chapter's conclusion is balanced. Mean reversion remains attractive because
it can produce frequent opportunities, strong statistical confidence, and
favorable Sharpe ratios. But those virtues coexist with a specific tail-risk
profile: rare, painful losses that often arrive after a long period of success.

The practical response is not to abandon mean reversion. It is to recognize
that strategies built on reversion need their own risk discipline, especially
around leverage, structural monitoring, and the possibility that a relationship
once thought stable may no longer hold.

# Review Checklist

- Does the strategy's attractive Sharpe ratio come from genuinely fast
  reversion, or from leverage applied to a fragile relationship?
- What structural change would invalidate the assumption that the spread or
  price should revert?
- Does the strategy scale in as losses grow, and if so, what prevents that from
  becoming uncontrolled?
- Are risk controls designed specifically for mean-reversion behavior rather
  than copied from trend-following logic?
- Is leverage low enough that a rare regime break is survivable?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [2.2 Cointegration]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/chapter-02/sections/02-cointegration/)
- Next: [Chapter overview]({{< meta site-url >}}/chapters/)
